meta.title: WebGL 2.0 Best Practices for SOTY Event Horizon
meta.author: Cleanlystudio
meta.description: Comprehensive WebGL optimization database for high-performance black hole visualization

minimize_draw_calls.description: Each draw call has CPU overhead for state setup and GPU command submission. Fewer calls = better performance.
minimize_draw_calls.target: Under 100 draw calls per frame for 60fps on mid-range hardware. Under 50 for mobile.

techniques.name: Geometry batching
techniques.description: Merge multiple geometries into a single buffer and draw with one call
techniques.implementation: Combine all static meshes into one BufferGeometry. Use different vertex attributes to distinguish objects.
techniques.savings: N objects → 1 draw call instead of N

techniques.name: Instanced rendering
techniques.description: Draw multiple copies of the same geometry with per-instance attributes (position, color, scale)
techniques.implementation: gl.drawArraysInstanced() / gl.drawElementsInstanced() with instance attribute buffers
techniques.ideal_for: Particle systems (10,000+ particles as one draw call), star fields, debris

techniques.name: Sort by material/shader
techniques.description: Group objects by their shader program to minimize program switches
techniques.implementation: Sort renderables by material ID before drawing. Front-to-back for opaque, back-to-front for transparent.
techniques.savings: Reduces program switch overhead (each switch costs ~0.1-0.5ms)

techniques.name: Indirect rendering
techniques.description: WebGL 2.0 does not support gl.drawArraysIndirect directly, but you can emulate with GPGPU
techniques.note: Store draw parameters in textures, read back to CPU, or use transform feedback

vertex_array_objects.description: VAOs store vertex attribute state so you don't need to reconfigure attributes every frame
vertex_array_objects.implementation: gl.createVertexArray() → gl.bindVertexArray() → configure attributes → unbind. At draw time: just bind the VAO.
vertex_array_objects.requirement: WebGL 2.0 has native VAO support. In WebGL 1.0, use the OES_vertex_array_object extension.
vertex_array_objects.savings: Eliminates per-frame attribute setup calls — significant for complex vertex layouts

uniform_buffer_objects.description: Share uniform data across multiple shader programs without redundant uploads
uniform_buffer_objects.implementation: gl.createBuffer() with UNIFORM_BUFFER target. gl.uniformBlockBinding() to connect.
uniform_buffer_objects.ideal_for: Camera matrices (view, projection) shared across all shaders. Scene globals (time, resolution, scroll position).
uniform_buffer_objects.savings: Upload once, use in all programs — eliminates redundant gl.uniformMatrix4fv() calls

rules: Cache current GL state (bound program, textures, blend mode) and skip redundant calls
Sort rendering order to minimize state changes: same program → same textures → same blend mode
Batch blend state: render all opaque objects first (no blend), then all transparent (with blend)
Minimize enable/disable calls — group objects by required GL state

power_of_two.description: Textures with dimensions that are powers of 2 (256, 512, 1024, 2048) have optimal GPU memory layout
power_of_two.webgl2_note: WebGL 2.0 supports NPOT textures with full mipmapping and wrapping, but POT is still faster on some hardware
power_of_two.recommendation: Use POT dimensions when possible, especially for frequently sampled textures

mipmapping.description: Pre-computed lower-resolution versions of textures for distant/small rendering
mipmapping.when_to_use: Any texture that will be viewed at varying distances (star textures, particle sprites, noise textures)
mipmapping.when_to_skip: Textures always viewed at 1:1 (UI elements, GPGPU data textures)

compressed_textures.recommendation: Use basis_universal or KTX2 container format — transcodes to the best format available on the device at load time
compressed_textures.savings: 4-8x reduction in GPU memory and texture upload time

texture_atlas.description: Combine multiple small textures into a single large texture, use UV coordinates to sample sub-regions
texture_atlas.ideal_for: Particle sprites (multiple particle types in one atlas), icon sets, HUD elements
texture_atlas.implementation: Pack textures into a 2048×2048 atlas. Pass UV offset/scale as attributes or uniforms.
texture_atlas.savings: Eliminates texture bind switching between particles of different types

rgba32f.description: 32-bit floating point per channel. Full precision GPGPU computation.
rgba32f.usage: Particle position/velocity storage, physics simulation, high-precision computation
rgba32f.extension: EXT_color_buffer_float (for rendering to) — required for GPGPU

rgba16f.description: 16-bit half-float per channel. Good precision for most visual purposes.
rgba16f.usage: Mobile GPGPU, HDR rendering, bloom/blur intermediate buffers
rgba16f.recommendation: Prefer RGBA16F over RGBA32F when possible — half the memory, adequate precision for most visual effects

nearest.description: No interpolation — pixel art look. Fastest.
nearest.use: GPGPU data textures (must read exact values), pixel art

linear.description: Bilinear interpolation. Smooth but no mip transitions.
linear.use: UI textures, textures viewed at constant size

linear_mipmap_linear.description: Trilinear — smoothest quality. Bilinear within mip + linear between mips.
linear_mipmap_linear.use: All textures viewed at varying distances — the default quality choice

anisotropic_filtering.description: Improves texture quality at oblique viewing angles
anisotropic_filtering.use: Accretion disk texture viewed at steep angle, floor/ground planes (if any)
anisotropic_filtering.performance: Minimal impact on modern GPUs — enable at max anisotropy for visual quality

minimize_render_target_switches.description: Switching render targets (FBOs) is one of the most expensive GL operations
strategy: Batch all renders to the same target together
Use MRT (Multiple Render Targets) to write multiple outputs in one pass
Plan your render pipeline to minimize target switches: Scene → Post-process chain → Screen

multiple_render_targets.description: WebGL 2.0 supports writing to multiple color attachments in a single fragment shader pass
multiple_render_targets.max_targets: Minimum 4 (query gl.MAX_COLOR_ATTACHMENTS for actual device support, typically 4-8)
multiple_render_targets.ideal_for: Deferred rendering — write position, normal, albedo, emission in one pass instead of four
multiple_render_targets.event_horizon_use: Write particle ID + depth in one pass for post-processing selection

rules: Bloom/glow: half resolution is visually identical (blur hides resolution loss)
GPGPU simulation: resolution = particle count, NOT screen resolution
Shadow maps: typically 1024×1024 or 2048×2048 regardless of screen size
DOF/motion blur: can run at 3/4 resolution and upsample

dynamic_resolution.description: Reduce render resolution when frame time exceeds budget, increase when headroom available
dynamic_resolution.implementation: Monitor frame time with performance.now(). If consistently > 14ms, reduce render scale by 10%. If < 10ms, increase.
dynamic_resolution.hysteresis: Only change after 10+ frames at the same threshold to avoid flickering

resource_disposal.description: WebGL resources (textures, buffers, programs, framebuffers) are NOT garbage collected. You MUST manually delete them.

three_js_disposal.important: Three.js dispose() calls the underlying GL delete. ALWAYS dispose when removing objects from scene.

vram_budget.mobile_budget: 128-512MB VRAM shared with system (varies by device)
vram_budget.monitoring: No direct WebGL API for VRAM usage. Use WEBGL_debug_renderer_info to identify GPU, then estimate.
vram_budget.estimation_formula: Total VRAM ≈ sum of (texture_width × texture_height × bytes_per_pixel × (1 + 0.33 if mipmapped)) + buffer_sizes + framebuffer_sizes

typed_array_pooling.description: Reuse Float32Array and other typed arrays instead of creating new ones each frame
typed_array_pooling.problem: new Float32Array(n) allocates memory that must be GC'd. In hot loops, this causes GC pauses (stuttering).
typed_array_pooling.solution: Pre-allocate arrays at initialization and reuse them. Zero them if needed with array.fill(0).
typed_array_pooling.example: const tempVec3 = new Float32Array(3); // reuse this everywhere instead of creating new ones

transform_feedback.description: Capture vertex shader output into buffers without going through fragment shader
transform_feedback.use_case: GPGPU particle simulation alternative to FBO ping-pong
transform_feedback.advantages: Simpler setup than FBO-based GPGPU for position/velocity updates
transform_feedback.disadvantages: Limited to vertex shader (no fragment shader compute), sequential output

integer_textures.description: Textures with integer formats (not normalized to 0-1)
integer_textures.use_case: Object ID buffers, index textures, lookup tables with exact integer values
integer_textures.event_horizon_use: Particle ID texture for per-particle selection and post-processing effects

3d_textures.use_case: 3D noise functions (pre-computed Perlin/Simplex noise), volumetric effects, LUT color grading
3d_textures.event_horizon_use: Pre-computed 3D noise texture for nebula/gas cloud rendering. Color grading LUT for cinematic post-processing.

uniform_buffer_objects_detail.description: Shared blocks of uniform data accessible by multiple programs
layout_rules: Vectors must be aligned to their size (vec4 → 16 byte alignment)
Matrices stored as arrays of column vectors
Padding may be inserted for alignment — be aware of memory layout

example_block.glsl: layout(std140) uniform SceneData { mat4 viewMatrix; mat4 projMatrix; vec4 cameraPosition; float time; float scrollProgress; };

non_power_of_two_textures.description: WebGL 2.0 fully supports NPOT textures with wrapping and mipmapping
non_power_of_two_textures.note: WebGL 1.0 limited NPOT textures to CLAMP_TO_EDGE wrapping and no mipmaps
non_power_of_two_textures.recommendation: Still prefer POT for performance-critical textures, but NPOT is fine for most use cases

gpu_identification.note: Some browsers block this for fingerprinting. Fallback to benchmarking.

capability_queries.use: Float texture render targets (required for GPGPU)

runtime_benchmarking.description: If GPU identification is blocked, run a quick benchmark at startup
runtime_benchmarking.technique: Render a known scene (e.g., 1000 particles) for 60 frames, measure average frame time

thresholds.medium: 4-10ms per frame → reduced particles, half-res bloom
thresholds.low: > 10ms per frame → minimal particles, no post-processing, reduced resolution

tiers.features: Full GPGPU particles, full post-processing, full resolution, all effects
tiers.target: Desktop with dedicated GPU, high-end mobile

tiers.requirement: WebGL 2.0 without float textures OR WebGL 1.0 with extensions
tiers.features: Reduced particles (CPU-computed), simplified post-processing, reduced effects
Minimal particles, no GPGPU, basic shaders, no post-processing
CSS 3D transforms for basic animation, pre-rendered video, static images with parallax
tiers.target: Browsers with WebGL disabled, very old devices

fallback_strategies.detection_code_pattern: 1. Try getContext('webgl2'). 2. If fail, try getContext('webgl'). 3. Check extensions. 4. Run benchmark. 5. Select tier.

common_pitfalls.pitfall: Creating textures or buffers every frame
common_pitfalls.symptom: Increasing memory usage, eventual crash or severe GC pauses
common_pitfalls.fix: Pre-allocate all resources at init. Reuse with sub-data updates.

common_pitfalls.pitfall: Not disposing resources on scene change
common_pitfalls.symptom: VRAM leak — performance degrades over time
common_pitfalls.fix: Track all GL resources. Dispose on unmount/navigation.

common_pitfalls.pitfall: Overdraw — rendering transparent layers that overlap extensively
common_pitfalls.symptom: Fragment shader executes multiple times per pixel, reducing performance
common_pitfalls.fix: Sort transparent objects back-to-front. Reduce number of overlapping transparent layers. Use premultiplied alpha.

common_pitfalls.symptom: GPU stall — CPU waits for GPU to finish before continuing
common_pitfalls.fix: Use PIXEL_PACK_BUFFER with gl.fenceSync() for async readback in WebGL 2.0

common_pitfalls.pitfall: High pixel ratio on high-DPI screens (4x pixels on Retina)
common_pitfalls.symptom: 4x more fragment shader work. Severe performance loss on mobile Retina.
30fps or lower on mobile despite working on desktop
common_pitfalls.fix: Mobile GPUs have 4-10x less fragment throughput. Reduce shader complexity (fewer texture reads, simpler math, lower precision).

profiling_tools.features: Capture and replay WebGL frames, inspect textures/buffers/state, identify redundant calls
Frame time breakdown (JS, Rendering, Painting, GPU), identify long tasks, memory profiling
Measure actual GPU time per draw call or render pass
profiling_tools.note: May be disabled in some browsers for security/fingerprinting reasons

profiling_tools.type: Frame debugger (requires running in debug context)
profiling_tools.features: Full frame capture, shader debugging, texture inspection, draw call breakdown
Monitor GL calls in real-time, detect redundant state changes
